original_abstract: The terms *fundamentalist* and *fundamentalism* were originally coined in 1920 within the Northern Baptist Convention during a period of intense theological friction caused by the rise of modernism. This article argues that these terms were initially embraced by defenders of orthodoxy as positive identifiers of their commitment to the "fundamentals" of the faith. However, almost immediately following their inception, both words became targets of criticism from more liberal Christian circles. Like many theological identifiers, they underwent rapid semantic shift, specifically through pejoration and lexical extension. By 1923, the label "fundamentalist" had already migrated into political journalism to describe rigid adherents of various secular ideologies. The most significant turning point—one that permanently etched these neologisms into the public consciousness across North America and the United Kingdom—was the 1925 Scopes "monkey trial." This event served as the apogee of the terms' transformation, forever linking "fundamentalism" with anti-intellectualism and obscurantism in the secular imagination.
humanized_abstract: The terms fundamentalist and fundamentalism were coined within the Northern Baptist Convention in 1920 to describe the more conservative theology of modernism. This entry discusses how the terms were used as labels for advocates of orthodoxy of the "fundamentals" of the faith, and how the terms were “assailed from all sides” by liberal Christians, falling prey to the processes of pejoration and lexical extension. By 1923 the term fundamentalist was appearing in political journalism to describe adherents of any number of rigid secular belief systems. It would not be until the 1925 Scopes monkey trial that the terms would be solidified as an ideological reference point in the secular mindset of the public in North America and the United Kingdom, with connotations of anti-intellectualism and obstinacy.
